title: Chapter 26: Night Market
content: The abandoned Meridian Street station lived up to its reputation.

Wren descended the broken escalator behind Kade and June, stepping over shattered tiles and heaps of desiccated advertising. The platform below was dark until it wasn't—a constellation of battery lanterns and chemical glowsticks, a sudden sprawl of light and noise in the belly of the city.
The Night Market.

Hundreds of invisibles moved between makeshift stalls constructed from salvaged metal and plastic sheeting. The air smelled like cooking oil and motor grease and the sharp chemical bite of stolen hand sanitizer. Voices overlapped in a wall of sound—bargaining, gossiping, laughing at jokes that only made sense if you'd been erased.
A woman Wren didn't recognize pressed a paper cup into her hand. "Soup. Three credits."
"Credits?"
Kade leaned in. "Favors, mostly. Services rendered. Sometimes information. The soup lady—her name's Dot. She'll take a door opened or a system glitched as payment."
Wren looked at the soup. It was brown and warm and it smelled like someone had tried.
She drank it.

The market operated on trust—the only currency that worked among people the banking system refused to acknowledge. No digital ledgers, no receipts. Just memory, and the understanding that cheating meant exile from the only community that could keep you alive.

Stalls offered everything: canned food, batteries, medication with labels in six languages, clothing sorted by size and condition, electronics in various states of disrepair. One vendor specialized in forged transit passes. Another traded in real maps of the Underlayer's shifting topology.

Information had its own row. Invisibles who'd been surface-side recently sat behind tables, telling anyone who'd listen what they'd seen. Corporate restructuring at NeuroScale. A new surveillance rollout in the financial district. A shelter that had stopped checking IDs.

Wren walked the rows slowly, absorbing the scale of it. The invisible economy wasn't survival—it was infrastructure. A system as complex as the one above, built on different rules but equally functional.
She hadn't known it was this big. This organized.
"You're gaping," June said, appearing at her elbow. The teenager's face was half-hidden by her hoodie, but her voice carried a smirk. "First time?"
"Is it that obvious?"
"You're looking at it like a tourist. The rest of us are looking at it like a grocery store."
Wren was still taking it in when Ash materialized beside her.

He hadn't been at the entrance when they arrived. She'd assumed he wasn't coming. But here he was, hands in his pockets, tattoos dim, watching the crowd with the flat assessment of a predator surveying territory.
"Stay close," he said.
"I can handle—"
"Stay close."
Something in his voice made her obey. Not fear—attunement. He was reading something in the market she couldn't see.

They wound through the stalls until they reached a section Wren hadn't noticed: a cluster of tables near the abandoned track, positioned apart from the main flow. Quieter. The people sitting there didn't hawk or bargain. They waited.
"That's the intel section," June explained, materializing again. "High-value information. Need an introduction to access it."
"Who gives introductions?"
"I do." June held out her hand. Ash looked at it, then at her, then sighed and handed over a protein bar from his pocket.
The girl grinned and led them to the farthest table.
The woman sitting behind it was unlike anyone Wren had seen in the Underlayer.

She was visible. Fully, unambiguously visible—clear skin, sharp features, auburn hair cut severe at the jaw. She wore a dark jacket too clean for the tunnels, and her nails were manicured. She looked like she'd walked out of a boardroom twenty minutes ago.
"This is Lira," June said. "She's—"
"I know what I am," Lira said. Her voice was low, precise, a voice trained to be heard in conference rooms. "I'm the one who got out."
Wren glanced at Ash. His expression had closed like a door slamming shut.

Lira noted the exchange. "He doesn't trust me. That's smart. I wouldn't trust me either." She leaned back in her chair. "But I have something you need, and that's the only currency that matters down here."
"What do you have?" Wren asked.

"NeuroScale. Internal access. Current protocols. Research trajectories. Deployment schedules." Lira ticked each item off on her fingers. "I spent four years in their regulatory compliance division. I know where the bodies are buried—metaphorically and, given what I've seen in the trial files, almost literally."
Ash stepped forward. "You're visible. You can walk into any building, access any system. Why aren't you above, living a normal life?"
Lira's expression flickered—brief, controlled, but Wren caught it. Pain behind the polish.

"Because I helped bury those bodies," she said quietly. "I signed the forms. Redacted the data. Stood in meetings and said 'adverse event rates are within acceptable parameters' while people were losing their minds, their identities, their existence. And then one day I realized the acceptable parameters were me."
She unbuttoned her left cuff and rolled her sleeve up to the elbow. The skin on her forearm was smooth, unmarked.
But when she concentrated—Wren saw it. A shimmer. A ripple at the edges, so faint it might have been a trick of the lantern light.

"Stage 0," Lira said. "Pre-clinical exposure. Low-dose, long-term. Environmental contamination in the lab. It's coming for me. Slower than the rest of you, but it's coming. In two years, maybe three, I'll be where you are now."
She rolled her sleeve back down.
"So I'm here. Because the only people who understand what's happening are the ones it's already happened to."
Ash's jaw worked. Wren could see the calculation behind his expression—running the odds, weighing the risk.
"You have documents?" he asked.
"I have access. The documents stay on NeuroScale servers. Safer that way—nothing to confiscate, nothing to lose. But I can pull specific files if you tell me what you need."
"Names. Trial subjects. Locations."
"Some of that I can get. Some of it is above my clearance."
"Vance. I want everything on Vance."
Lira's composure cracked—just a hairline fracture. "Vance is the reason I left. What I know about her could fill a book. What I can prove would fit on a napkin."
"Then get me more."
"It's not that simple. She's paranoid. Layered security. Biometric locks I can't bypass remotely."
Ash leaned over the table. "Try harder."
Lira looked at him. Then at Wren.
"He's always like this?" she asked.
"Sometimes he's more intense," Wren said.

A ghost of a smile from Lira. The first real expression she'd shown. "I like her. She has perspective." She stood, gathered a slim bag from behind her chair. "I'll be back at the next market. Bring questions. I'll bring answers."
She walked away, weaving through the crowd with the easy confidence of someone the world still acknowledged.
Wren watched her go. "What do you think?"
Ash's silence was answer enough.
"Because I think she might be telling the truth," Wren pressed. "Stage 0 contamination—that's consistent with what you told me about the lab environment."
"Consistent doesn't mean true."
"Neither does suspicious."
Ash turned to face her. His tattoos pulsed—agitated, faster than his usual rhythm.
"Someone like her—visible, connected, still with access—doesn't give that up to live in a tunnel. Not out of guilt. Guilt doesn't work that way."
"How does guilt work?"
"It makes you confess. It doesn't make you strategize." He gestured toward where Lira had disappeared. "That woman is playing a long game. And we're not the ones she's playing it for."
Maybe. Maybe not.

Wren had spent enough time in corporate environments to recognize the calculus of self-interest. Lira had it—the controlled presentation, the measured disclosures, the strategic vulnerability. It was a performance. Wren had given the same performance a hundred times in meetings.
But performances weren't inherently lies. Sometimes they were just survival.
The market swirled around them. Voices, bartering, the scrape of boots on tile. Someone had started playing music—a battered radio, something old and synthetic and weirdly beautiful.
"We'll see," Wren said. "Next market."
"We'll see," Ash agreed. But his gaze stayed fixed on the dark end of the platform where Lira had vanished, and his tattoos burned low and steady, the color of a warning.
